ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Thai boat noodle soup with crispy fish. FOODON_00002473 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0FHR (Noodle soup) is the closest parent; no specific Thai boat noodle soup code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of crispy fish boat noodle soup weighing approximately 450 g, comprising noodles, broth, crispy fish pieces, and vegetables.

nutrient_source.title: Boat noodle soup (Kuay Tiao Ruea) — Thai noodle soup composite estimate derived from FAO/INFOODS SEA Food Composition Table for South East Asia and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for component ingredients (rice noodles, fried fish, pork-blood broth, vegetables)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai crispy fish boat noodle soup as a composite dish. Values are estimated by weighted ingredient composition: approximately 35% rice noodles (cooked), 15% crispy fried fish, 40% broth (pork-blood based, dark soy, fish sauce), 10% vegetables and garnish. FAO/INFOODS SEA values used for rice noodles and vegetables; USDA SR Legacy used for fried fish and broth components. Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce and dark soy sauce in the broth.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles (cooked) as analogous base; Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J. Am J Clin Nutr. 2021
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Thai crispy fish boat noodle soup. GI estimated at 40 based on cooked rice noodles (GI ~40–53 per University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021 international tables). The broth, fish, and vegetable components contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (40 × 44.1) / 100 ≈ 17.6, where carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 9.8 × 4.5 = 44.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce, dark soy sauce, and seasoned broth (~480 mg sodium per 100 g; ~2160 mg per bowl)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to very high sodium content from fish sauce and soy-based broth, which can significantly exceed the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg in a single serving.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~17.6 per bowl) from rice noodles; low GI base but portion size elevates total glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes can consume this dish in controlled portions, as the rice noodle base has a low GI (~40), but the moderate glycemic load per bowl warrants portion awareness and pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables to blunt postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate saturated fat from fried fish and broth; sodium content is the primary cardiovascular risk driver
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should exercise caution with this dish due to high sodium content (~2160 mg per bowl), which is associated with increased blood pressure and cardiovascular risk; the saturated fat contribution from crispy fried fish is moderate and less concerning than the sodium load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crispy Fish Boat Noodle Soup (Thai: ก๋วยเตี๋ยวเรือปลากรอบ, romanized: Kuay Tiao Ruea Pla Krob) is a Thai noodle soup dish served at Saap Saap Thai restaurant in Singapore. It is a variation of the classic Thai boat noodle (Kuay Tiao Ruea), a street-food staple originating from the canal-boat vendors of central Thailand. The dish features springy rice noodles submerged in a rich, dark, intensely flavoured broth made with pork blood, dark soy sauce, fish sauce, and aromatic spices. The defining element is the crispy fried fish — typically battered or seasoned white fish deep-fried until golden and crunchy — which provides textural contrast to the soft noodles and savoury broth. Garnishes typically include bean sprouts, morning glory (water spinach), fried garlic, dried chilli flakes, and fresh herbs. Nutritionally, the dish provides approximately 82 kcal per 100 g, with moderate protein (~5.8 g/100 g) from the fish and broth, low fat (~2.4 g/100 g), and moderate carbohydrate (~9.8 g/100 g) from the rice noodles. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low), based on the rice noodle base, though the glycemic load per bowl is moderate (~17.6) due to portion size. The primary clinical concern is the very high sodium content (~480 mg/100 g; ~2160 mg per bowl) from fish sauce and soy-based broth, making it a high-concern dish for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For people with Type 2 Diabetes, the low GI base is favourable but portion control is advised. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to pork blood in the broth, and contains fish and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Thai crispy fish boat noodle soup as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated from weighted ingredient composition using FAO/INFOODS SEA and USDA SR Legacy component data. GI is estimated from the closest analogous food (cooked rice noodles) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component-level sources are high quality and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reliably reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA or USDA SR Legacy component sources used for this composite estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the FAO/INFOODS SEA source for this dish type. Trans fat (trans_fat_g) is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in deep-fried fish and rice noodle components per USDA SR Legacy. The dish contains pork blood in the traditional broth and is therefore not halal. Allergens include fish and soy (from fish sauce and dark soy sauce); gluten may be present from soy sauce. GI estimated at 40 based on cooked rice noodles as the primary glycemic carbohydrate source (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2021). Sodium is elevated due to fish sauce and dark soy sauce; a single bowl (~450 g) provides approximately 2160 mg sodium, which exceeds the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg. The menu code N13 is a restaurant-specific identifier from Saap Saap Thai - Funan, Singapore (field survey data listing).
